name: com.coachfit.auth.adapter.in.GoogleOAuthController
comment:
Controller for Google OAuth2 OIDC flow. 
 GET /api/v1/auth/oauth/google           → 302 redirect to Google auth page GET /api/v1/auth/oauth/google/callback  → exchange code, issue JWT, redirect to frontend

State (CSRF protection)

A 16-byte random hex state is generated on initiation and stored in Redis with a 5-minute TTL. The callback verifies the state is present (was generated by this server) before proceeding.

name: com.coachfit.auth.adapter.in.GoogleOAuthProperties
comment: Binds Google OAuth2 callback URL from {@code app.google.callback-url}. Used by both {@link GoogleOAuthController} (for building the auth URL) and {@link com.coachfit.auth.application.service.GoogleOAuthService}.

name: com.coachfit.auth.adapter.in.StravaOAuthController
comment:
Controller for Strava OAuth2 authorization-code flow. 
 GET /api/v1/auth/oauth/strava           → 302 redirect to Strava auth page GET /api/v1/auth/oauth/strava/callback  → exchange code, issue JWT, redirect to frontend

State (CSRF protection)

A 16-byte random hex state is generated on initiation and stored in Redis with a 5-minute TTL. The callback verifies the state is present (was generated by this server) before proceeding. See docs/08-auth-model.md §Security Checklist.

Error handling

Any errors (invalid state, Strava API failure) redirect to {@code /auth/error} on the frontend with a {@code reason} query param — never expose stack traces in OAuth redirects.

methods.name: initiateOAuth
methods.comment:
Redirects the browser to Strava's OAuth authorization page.

Docs/06-sync-engine-spec.md §Strava OAuth Flow step 2: "Redirect → https://www.strava.com/oauth/authorize?client_id=...&scope=activity:read_all..."

methods.name: handleCallback
methods.comment:
Handles the Strava callback after user authorization.

Docs/06-sync-engine-spec.md §Strava OAuth Flow steps 3–5: "User authorize → redirect back with code → Backend exchange code → access_token + refresh_token → Store encrypted tokens in oauth_connections"

name: com.coachfit.auth.adapter.out.persistence.AesTokenEncryptionUtil
comment:
AES-256-GCM encryption utility for OAuth tokens stored in the database.

docs/08-auth-model.md §Security Checklist: "OAuth tokens — AES-256-GCM encrypted in DB. Key in env var {@code OAUTH_ENCRYPTION_KEY}."

Format

Ciphertext is stored as: {@code Base64(12-byte IV || ciphertext || 16-byte GCM tag)}. The IV is randomly generated per encryption — never reused.

Key derivation

The configured string is hashed with SHA-256 to produce a stable 32-byte key, allowing any-length passphrase without padding/truncation issues.

methods.name: encrypt
methods.comment: Encrypts {@code plaintext} and returns a Base64 string safe for DB storage. Returns {@code null} if {@code plaintext} is {@code null}.

methods.name: decrypt
methods.comment: Decrypts a Base64-encoded ciphertext produced by {@link #encrypt}. Returns {@code null} if {@code encoded} is {@code null}.

name: com.coachfit.auth.adapter.out.persistence.ApiKeyEntity
comment: JPA entity mapping the {@code api_keys} table. Raw API key is never stored — only the SHA-256 hex hash and display prefix.

name: com.coachfit.auth.adapter.out.persistence.ApiKeyJpaRepository
comment: Spring Data JPA repository for {@link ApiKeyEntity}.

name: com.coachfit.auth.adapter.out.persistence.ApiKeyPersistenceAdapter
comment:
JPA adapter implementing {@link ApiKeyPersistencePort}.

{@code findActiveByHash} updates {@code last_used_at} inside the same transaction so every verified call is transparently tracked.

name: com.coachfit.auth.adapter.out.persistence.OAuthConnectionPersistenceAdapter
comment:
JdbcClient-based adapter for {@code oauth_connections} table.

Uses native SQL with {@code ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE} to handle both first-time connect and token refresh (e.g. user reconnects same Google account).

JdbcClient is used instead of JPA to avoid the PostgreSQL {@code TEXT[]} scopes column complexity and to keep the UPSERT atomic.

name: com.coachfit.auth.adapter.out.persistence.UserEntity
comment: JPA entity mapping the {@code users} table. Only the columns needed by the auth module are mapped here.

name: com.coachfit.auth.adapter.out.persistence.UserPersistenceAdapter
comment:
JPA + JdbcClient adapter implementing {@link UserPersistencePort}.

JPA is used for user CRUD. {@link JdbcClient} is used for subscription operations (cross-module table) to avoid importing JPA entities from the subscription module.

name: com.coachfit.auth.application.service.GarminOAuthService
comment:
Implements the Garmin Health API OAuth 1.0a three-legged authorization flow.

Flow (docs/08-auth-model.md §Garmin OAuth (Phase 1), docs/06-sync-engine-spec.md §OAuth 1.0a Flow):

  1. POST /oauth-service/oauth/request_token → temporary oauth_token + oauth_token_secret
  2. Redirect user to https://connect.garmin.com/oauthConfirm?oauth_token=...
  3. User authorizes → Garmin POSTs back to callback with oauth_token + oauth_verifier
  4. POST /oauth-service/oauth/access_token → permanent access_token + access_token_secret
  5. Store AES-256-GCM encrypted tokens in {@code oauth_connections}

OAuth 1.0a tokens do NOT expire — no refresh flow is required. The {@code access_token_secret} is stored in the {@code access_token_secret} column and needed for every HMAC-SHA1 signed request to Garmin.

HMAC-SHA1 signing: implemented here using standard JDK {@link Mac} — no extra OAuth library dependency required.

See docs/06-sync-engine-spec.md §Garmin Health API Integration.

methods.name: initiateOAuth
methods.comment:
Obtains a temporary request token from Garmin's OAuth service.

Sends a signed POST to {@code /oauth-service/oauth/request_token} with {@code oauth_callback} set to our redirect URI. Garmin responds with a URL-encoded body: {@code oauth_token=...&oauth_token_secret=...&oauth_callback_confirmed=true}.

methods.name: handleCallback
methods.comment:
Exchanges the temporary credentials for permanent access tokens, then resolves or creates the CoachFit user and stores the encrypted tokens.

Sends a signed POST to {@code /oauth-service/oauth/access_token} using the request token + verifier. Garmin responds with the permanent {@code access_token} and {@code access_token_secret}.

The {@code access_token_secret} is stored encrypted in the {@code access_token_secret} column of {@code oauth_connections}. It is required for every subsequent signed request.

OAuth 1.0a tokens never expire (docs/08-auth-model.md §Garmin OAuth).

methods.name: exchangeForAccessToken
methods.comment: Calls Garmin's access_token endpoint with the request token + verifier. Returns the permanent access_token and access_token_secret.

methods.name: resolveUser
methods.comment:
Resolves the CoachFit user for a Garmin connection.

Garmin does not provide an email or profile in the OAuth response. We use the {@code access_token} (the Garmin user access token) as the {@code provider_user_id} — Garmin embeds it in every push payload as {@code userAccessToken}, which is how we correlate push data to CoachFit users.

Resolution order:

  1. Existing Garmin connection by userAccessToken → load user
  2. No match → auto-register a new CoachFit account (no password, no email yet)

methods.name: buildOAuthHeader
methods.signature: buildOAuthHeader(java.lang.String, java.lang.String, java.lang.String, java.lang.String, java.lang.String, java.lang.String, java.util.Map)
methods.comment:
Builds the OAuth 1.0a {@code Authorization} header using HMAC-SHA1 signing.

Algorithm (RFC 5849 §3):

  1. Collect all oauth_* parameters + any request body params
  2. Percent-encode and sort them
  3. Build the Signature Base String: METHOD&encoded_url&encoded_params
  4. Sign with HMAC-SHA1 using key = consumerSecret&tokenSecret
  5. Append oauth_signature to the Authorization header

methods.name: buildSignatureBaseString
methods.comment:
Builds the OAuth 1.0a Signature Base String (RFC 5849 §3.4.1).

Format: {@code METHOD&percent_encoded_base_url&percent_encoded_normalized_params}

methods.name: hmacSha1Base64
methods.comment: Signs the given data with HMAC-SHA1 and returns the Base64-encoded result.

methods.name: encode
methods.comment: RFC 3986 percent-encoding (stricter than {@link java.net.URLEncoder}).

methods.name: parseUrlEncoded
methods.comment: Parses a URL-encoded response body into a key→value map.

methods.name: generateNonce
methods.comment: Generates a random nonce (16-byte hex string).

methods.name: currentTimestamp
methods.comment: Returns the current Unix epoch time in seconds as a string.

methods.name: maskToken
methods.comment: Masks a token for safe logging — shows first 8 chars only.

name: com.coachfit.auth.application.service.GarminOAuthService$AccessTokens
comment: Permanent Garmin access credentials returned after the verifier exchange.

name: com.coachfit.auth.application.service.GoogleOAuthService
comment:
Implements the Google OAuth2 OIDC login/register flow.

Flow (docs/08-auth-model.md §Google OAuth):

  1. Build authorization URL from Spring's {@link ClientRegistration}
  2. Exchange authorization code for tokens at Google's token endpoint
  3. Fetch user info from Google's userinfo endpoint
  4. Find existing OAuth connection → load user
  5. If not found: look up by email → link Google to existing account
  6. If no existing account: auto-register (no password)
  7. Store/update OAuth connection (encrypted tokens)
  8. Issue CoachFit JWT + refresh token

methods.name: buildAuthorizationUrl
methods.comment: Builds the Google authorization URL to redirect the browser to.

name: com.coachfit.auth.application.service.StravaOAuthService
comment:
Implements the Strava OAuth2 authorization-code flow.

Flow (docs/06-sync-engine-spec.md §Strava OAuth Flow, docs/08-auth-model.md §Strava OAuth):

  1. Build authorization URL with required scopes
  2. Exchange authorization code for access + refresh tokens
  3. Extract athlete profile from Strava token response
  4. Resolve CoachFit user (find by provider ID → find by email → auto-register)
  5. Store/update OAuth connection (AES-256-GCM encrypted tokens)
  6. Issue CoachFit JWT + refresh token

Note: Strava returns the athlete object as part of the token-exchange response, so a separate profile API call is not needed.

methods.name: buildAuthorizationUrl
methods.comment: Builds the Strava authorization URL the browser will be redirected to.

methods.name: resolveUser
methods.comment:
Resolves the CoachFit user from the Strava token response using three-step lookup:
  1. Existing oauth_connection for this Strava athlete ID → load user
  2. Existing user with the same email → link Strava to that account
  3. No match → auto-register a new CoachFit account (no password)
See docs/08-auth-model.md §Strava OAuth.

methods.name: exchangeCode
methods.comment: Exchanges the authorization code for Strava access + refresh tokens. Strava returns the athlete summary as part of the token response.

name: com.coachfit.sync.adapter.in.StravaJobConsumer
comment:
Redis Stream consumer for the {@code sync:jobs} stream.

Implements the worker side of docs/06-sync-engine-spec.md §Redis Stream Design:

  • Consumer group: {@code sync-workers}
  • Consumer name: {@code worker-1} (single consumer for MVP)
  • Batch: up to 10 messages per poll
  • Retry: up to {@value MAX_ATTEMPTS} times with re-enqueue; then dead-letter
  • Pending recovery: every 5 minutes, reclaims messages idle >60s

Scheduling is enabled via {@link EnableScheduling} on this component. For MVP, a single consumer is sufficient; additional consumers can be added to the same group for horizontal scaling.

methods.name: initConsumerGroup
methods.comment: Creates the consumer group on startup if it doesn't exist. Uses {@code MKSTREAM} to also create the stream key if absent.

methods.name: pollJobs
methods.comment:
Polls the {@code sync:jobs} stream every second for new messages.

Uses {@code XREADGROUP} with {@code >} (new messages not yet delivered). Acknowledgement ({@code XACK}) only happens after successful processing.

methods.name: recoverPendingMessages
methods.comment:
Every 5 minutes: reclaims messages that have been pending for more than 60 seconds. This handles the case where the worker crashed mid-processing.

Per spec: "Scheduled job check XPENDING every 5 minutes, reclaim messages idle >60s".

name: com.coachfit.sync.application.service.StravaActivitySyncService
comment:
Worker service: processes a single Strava activity sync job from the Redis queue.

Pipeline (docs/06-sync-engine-spec.md §Strava Webhook Processing Step 2 Worker):

  1. Load & decrypt Strava tokens
  2. Refresh token if expired
  3. Fetch activity summary from Strava API
  4. Fetch time-series streams from Strava API
  5. Source-level dedup check ({@code UNIQUE(user_id, 'strava', activity_id)})
  6. For create events: normalize → store activity + streams + laps
  7. For update events: merge updated fields onto existing row
  8. For delete events: soft-delete the activity
  9. Calculate NP / IF / TSS and update the stored row
  10. Log sync result

methods.name: process
methods.comment: Processes a single Strava sync job.

methods.name: getValidAccessToken
methods.comment: Returns a valid access token. Refreshes if expired or expiry is within 60 seconds.

methods.name: normalizeSport
methods.comment: Maps Strava sport type to CoachFit internal sport string. Strava uses "sport_type" (new) or "type" (legacy) — prefer sport_type.

name: com.coachfit.sync.application.service.StravaWebhookService
comment:
Implements the fast path of the Strava webhook lifecycle:
  1. Webhook subscription verification (GET)
  2. Event receipt + Redis enqueue (POST) — must return <2s

All heavy processing (token refresh, API fetch, persistence) is delegated to {@link StravaActivitySyncService} via the async queue worker.

See docs/06-sync-engine-spec.md §Strava Webhook Processing.

methods.name: verify
methods.comment:
Verifies Strava's subscription challenge.

Strava sends: {@code GET /webhooks/strava?hub.mode=subscribe&hub.verify_token=...&hub.challenge=...}

methods.name: handleEvent
methods.comment:
Handles an incoming Strava webhook event.

Only {@code object_type=activity} events with {@code aspect_type in (create, update, delete)} trigger downstream processing. All other events are acknowledged silently (200).

Per spec: must return <2s — only Redis enqueue happens synchronously.
